concepts.name: Fault Tolerance
concepts.description: Designing systems where failures are expected, detected, isolated, and recovered from automatically without losing progress.

concepts.name: Failure Rate
concepts.description: The expected frequency of component failures, typically measured in Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) or Failures In Time (FIT).

concepts.name: Young-Daly Checkpoint Law
concepts.description: A formula calculating the optimal checkpoint interval by balancing the cost of writing checkpoints against the expected cost of reworking lost progress.

concepts.name: Transient Failures
concepts.description: Temporary hardware disruptions like single-event upsets or voltage fluctuations that cause incorrect computations without permanent hardware damage.

concepts.name: Fail-Stop Failures
concepts.description: Failures where a component ceases operation entirely and detectably, such as a node crash or network partition.

concepts.name: Byzantine Failures
concepts.description: Insidious failures where a component continues operating but produces incorrect results, violating the assumption of correct execution.

concepts.name: Silent Data Corruption
concepts.description: A type of Byzantine failure where memory or computation errors produce wrong values without triggering explicit hardware or software exceptions.

concepts.name: Correlated Failures
concepts.description: Simultaneous failures of multiple components due to shared dependencies like power supplies or network switches.

concepts.name: Failure Domains
concepts.description: Hierarchical boundaries (e.g., node, rack, datacenter) used to isolate redundant components and prevent correlated failures.

concepts.name: Bathtub Curve
concepts.description: A reliability model describing how hardware failure rates vary across infant mortality, useful life, and wear-out phases.

concepts.name: Error Correcting Codes
concepts.description: Hardware-level mechanisms that add redundant bits to memory or transmissions to detect and correct transient bit flips.

concepts.name: Hardware Redundancy
concepts.description: Using component duplication and voting circuits, like Double Modular Redundancy, to detect and mask faults during computation.

concepts.name: Fault Injection
concepts.description: The engineering discipline of deliberately introducing faults into a system to empirically verify robustness mechanisms.

concepts.name: Fault Model
concepts.description: A definition of how a physical hardware fault manifests itself in the system in terms of duration, location, and granularity.

concepts.name: Error Model
concepts.description: A representation of how a localized fault propagates and alters the higher-level behavior of an ML system.

concepts.name: Checkpointing
concepts.description: Periodically writing the state of a model, optimizer, and training loop to durable storage to preserve progress.

concepts.name: Synchronous Checkpointing
concepts.description: A mechanism where all workers coordinate to reach a consistent state and pause training while data is written to storage.

concepts.name: Asynchronous Checkpointing
concepts.description: A method where workers snapshot their state in memory and continue training while a background process writes to persistent storage.

concepts.name: Centralized Checkpointing
concepts.description: A simple distributed checkpointing approach where all workers send their state to a single coordinator process.

concepts.name: Distributed Checkpointing
concepts.description: An approach where each worker writes its own portion of the checkpoint directly to shared storage, avoiding a central network bottleneck.

concepts.name: Sharded Checkpointing
concepts.description: A distributed checkpointing method where each worker writes only its assigned parameter and optimizer shard.

concepts.name: Cold Restart
concepts.description: A recovery procedure where all processes are killed and the entire cluster state is reloaded from persistent storage.

concepts.name: Warm Restart
concepts.description: A recovery procedure that preserves the in-memory state of surviving workers and only replaces the failed nodes.

concepts.name: Straggler Mitigation
concepts.description: Strategies to detect and handle slow but functionally correct workers that bottleneck synchronous distributed training.

concepts.name: Elastic Training
concepts.description: Allowing a training job to dynamically resize and redistribute data when the number of available workers changes.

concepts.name: Stateless Serving
concepts.description: An inference architecture where each request is independent and no per-session context is maintained across requests.

concepts.name: Stateful Serving
concepts.description: An inference architecture that maintains context across a sequence of requests, complicating failover and recovery.

concepts.name: Active-Active Replication
concepts.description: A serving redundancy strategy where all replicas actively process requests and load is distributed among them.

concepts.name: Active-Passive Replication
concepts.description: A serving redundancy strategy where primary replicas handle all traffic while standby replicas sit idle awaiting a failover event.

concepts.name: KV Cache Checkpointing
concepts.description: Periodically saving the conversational key-value cache of an LLM session to avoid full regeneration upon replica failure.
